question: Your team has fine-tuned a generative AI model for summarization tasks. How would you design an evaluation framework to compare its outputs against a baseline model?
answer: To evaluate a fine-tuned summarization model against a baseline, I would design a mixed-methods framework combining automated metrics, human evaluation, and task-specific tests. First, create a diverse test set of 500-1000 examples covering multiple domains (news, scientific articles, dialogues) and summary lengths. The baseline should be a strong open-source model like BART or T5 of similar size to control for capacity differences. For automated metrics, use ROUGE-1/2/L to measure n-gram overlap, BLEU for precision, and BERTScore for semantic similarity using embeddings. These provide quick, reproducible signals but correlate imperfectly with human judgment. Next, conduct human evaluation with at least 3 annotators per example, rating summaries on fluency (grammatical correctness), faithfulness (factual consistency with the source), coherence (logical flow), and relevance (information selection). Use a Likert scale of 1-5. For faithfulness, include specific contradiction detection using NLI models like DeBERTa to flag factual errors automatically. Additionally, run task-specific tests: measure compression ratio (target 20-30% of original length), extractive overlap percentage, and latency under production-like conditions. Finally, apply statistical significance testing (paired bootstrap or Wilcoxon signed-rank) on all metrics to determine if differences are meaningful. Report both point estimates and confidence intervals. This framework catches both surface-level quality improvements and real-world utility gains, while automated metrics flag regressions quickly and human evaluation validates real quality.
explanation: Evaluation of generative models is fundamentally harder than discriminative models because quality is subjective and there is no single ground truth. ROUGE and BLEU were designed for machine translation and summarization respectively, but they only measure n-gram overlap and can miss paraphrases or penalize creative rewrites. BERTScore partially addresses this by using contextual embeddings, but it still fails to capture factual hallucinations—a summary might have high semantic similarity yet contain a critical factual error. This is why human evaluation remains the gold standard, especially for faithfulness. In practice, at Google, teams use a combination of ROUGE for regression testing on every checkpoint and periodic expert human evaluations for release decisions. Meta's Llama evaluation includes both automated safety benchmarks and human preference ratings. The key edge case is domain shift: a model fine-tuned on CNN/DailyMail summarization may perform well on that dataset but fail on scientific abstracts due to different discourse structures. Another common pitfall is annotator disagreement—inter-annotator agreement (Krippendorff's alpha) should be measured and low-agreement items reviewed. Real-world usage also requires evaluating robustness: how does the model handle input noise (typos, grammatical errors), out-of-distribution topics, or very long documents? A comprehensive evaluation framework must account for all these dimensions to avoid deploying a model that looks good on paper but fails in production.
diagram:
flowchart TD
  A[Collect diverse test dataset] --> B[Run baseline model]
  B --> C[Run fine-tuned model]
  C --> D[Compute automated metrics\nROUGE-1/2/L, BLEU, BERTScore]
  D --> E[Human annotation\nfluency, faithfulness, coherence, relevance]
  E --> F[Statistical comparison\nsignificance test on all metrics]
  F --> G[Report point estimates & confidence intervals]

question: How would you design a simple AI agent that can answer user questions by searching a knowledge base and performing calculations, using an LLM as its reasoning core?
answer: A simple AI agent with search and calculation capabilities follows a loop: receive user input, have the LLM decide on an action (search, calculate, or respond), execute that action, feed the result back to the LLM, and repeat until the LLM gives a final answer. The core architecture has three components: a system prompt defining available tools with their schemas, a tool execution layer, and a conversation loop that maintains message history. The system prompt tells the LLM it has access to functions like search_knowledge_base(query: string) and calculate(expression: string), specifying the function names, parameters, and return types. When the user asks something like 'What is the population of Tokyo times 2?', the LLM first calls search_knowledge_base with 'population of Tokyo', returning 37 million. The agent appends this tool result as a message and calls the LLM again. The LLM then calls calculate with '37 million * 2', returning 74 million. On the third turn, the LLM produces a final answer: 'The population of Tokyo is approximately 37 million. Doubled, that would be 74 million.' The loop terminates when the model generates a response without tool calls. This pattern uses function calling or tool use APIs available in most LLM providers like OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google. The agent must handle errors gracefully — if search returns nothing, the LLM should ask clarifying questions. Rate limiting, timeout handling, and context window management are critical in production. The agent should also truncate or summarize long tool outputs to stay within context limits. This design is used in real-world systems like customer support bots that look up order details then process refunds, or data analysis assistants that query databases then format results.
explanation: This question tests foundational understanding of the LLM-as-reasoning-engine pattern that underlies nearly all production AI agent systems. The key insight is that the LLM is not executing code directly — it is generating structured outputs (tool calls) that the runtime interprets and executes. This separation of reasoning from execution is what makes agents safe and debuggable. The agent loop is essentially a reactive state machine: the LLM's output determines the next transition. A common pitfall is not handling the case where the LLM returns a malformed tool call or hallucinates a function that doesn't exist. Real implementations must validate tool call schemas before execution. Another edge case is tool call chaining — the agent might attempt millions of nested operations if unbounded, so production systems cap the number of iterations (typically 10-25). Rate limiting is crucial because each loop iteration is an API call with cost and latency. Companies like Stripe use agents for fraud detection workflows where the agent calls data lookup tools then applies business rules. Uber uses agent patterns in their support system where a bot searches trip history and calculates fare adjustments. Airbnb uses multi-tool agents for their smart pricing system that pulls market data, applies pricing formulas, and updates listings. The design must also handle tool execution failures — if the knowledge base is down, the agent should attempt a retry or gracefully degrade. Security considerations include preventing prompt injection where user input tricks the agent into calling dangerous tools. Solutions include isolating tool definitions in the system prompt and validating all tool call parameters. The pattern generalizes to more complex agentic systems like coding agents that call read/write file tools, or research agents that browse websites and compile reports.
diagram:
flowchart TD
  User[User Input] --> LLM[LLM Reasoning: Decide Action]
  LLM --> IsTool{LLM Requests Tool?}
  IsTool -->|Yes| ToolCall[Parse Tool Call]
  ToolCall --> Validate{Valid Schema?}
  Validate -->|No| Error[Return Error to LLM]
  Error --> LLM
  Validate -->|Yes| Execute[Run Tool Function]
  Execute --> Result[Append Result to Messages]
  Result --> LLM
  IsTool -->|No| Response[Generate Final Answer]
  Response --> User

question: How does speculative decoding reduce per-token latency during autoregressive LLM inference, and what are the critical failure modes that can cause it to degrade performance in production?
answer: Speculative decoding accelerates autoregressive inference by having a smaller draft model generate multiple candidate tokens per step, which the target language model then verifies in a single forward pass. Standard autoregressive decoding is memory-bandwidth-bound because each generated token requires loading all model parameters from HBM into on-chip SRAM while performing relatively few FLOPs. Speculative decoding exploits this by processing K candidate positions with one forward pass of the target model, using the causal attention mask to compute logits for all K positions simultaneously. The draft model proposes tokens cheaply, for example a 100M-parameter model serving as draft for a 7B target. The target then computes acceptance probabilities via rejection sampling: for each candidate token t_i from draft distribution q(t|x), the target compares its distribution p(t|x) and accepts with probability min(1, p(t_i)/q(t_i)). Expected tokens per step equals sum_{i=0}^{K-1} acceptance_rate^i, capped at K. With acceptance rate 0.8 and K=8, this yields roughly 4.5 tokens per step on average. Critical failure modes include: distribution mismatch where a poorly calibrated draft model drops acceptance rates below 0.5 and the overhead of running both models exceeds baseline latency; batch size effects where speculative decoding is less beneficial at high batch sizes because the memory-bandwidth bottleneck is amortized differently; tokenizer mismatch requiring identical vocabulary between draft and target; cold start where generating the first K tokens has no speedup benefit; and variable acceptance rate across positions where early positions accept more frequently, making an excessively large K waste computation. Production deployments use adaptive K selection based on running acceptance rate estimates and fall back to standard decoding when conditions are unfavorable. The technique is mathematically guaranteed to preserve the target model's output distribution because rejection sampling corrects every divergence.
explanation: Speculative decoding is significant because it provides lossless acceleration: the output distribution is identical to running the target model alone, unlike quantization or pruning which introduce approximation. This property comes from the rejection sampling correction mechanism, a fundamental Monte Carlo method. The technique is most effective in latency-sensitive interactive applications where batch size is small, typically 1 to 4, such as chat interfaces, code completion, and real-time agents, where 2x to 5x speedups are common. Edge cases include tasks requiring highly specific domain knowledge where a generic draft model fails, causing regression to baseline performance; the impact of sequence length on KV cache memory which affects the feasibility of running two models simultaneously on GPU; and interaction with other optimizations like continuous batching and PagedAttention. At Cloudflare, speculative decoding is relevant for Workers AI edge inference where GPU memory is constrained and models must share resources. Databricks uses it in MosaicML model serving to improve throughput-per-dollar for large model inference. Snowflake's Cortex AI applies it to reduce latency for SQL generation and document summarization. An advanced consideration is multi-draft speculative decoding where multiple draft models propose candidates in parallel and the target verifies them using tree attention, extending the technique to domains with heterogeneous inputs. Understanding speculative decoding demonstrates a deep grasp of the transformer's memory-bound inference profile, the mathematical properties of rejection sampling, and the practical engineering constraints of production ML systems.
diagram:
flowchart TD
  A[Input Sequence] --> B[Draft Model Proposes K Tokens]
  B --> C[Target Model Verifies All K Positions]
  C --> D{Rejection Sampling: p_target >= q_draft?}
  D -->|Accept| E[Keep Token]
  D -->|Reject| F[Drop Token and Remaining Candidates]
  E --> G{More Tokens Needed?}
  F --> G
  G -->|Yes| B
  G -->|No| H[Return Final Output]

question: Explain the KV cache in transformer-based LLMs: what problem does it solve, how is it implemented, and what are the key memory and computational trade-offs when serving long-context requests?
answer: The KV cache stores the Key and Value tensors from each self-attention layer across previous decoding steps, solving the problem of redundant computation during autoregressive generation. Without it, generating each new token would require re-running the full multi-head attention over every previous token from scratch, giving O(n²) compute per step and O(n³) total for an n-token sequence. With the cache, only the new token's K and V need to be computed at each step, reducing per-step compute to O(1) attention relative to sequence length. Implementation: during prefill (prompt ingestion), all prompt tokens' K and V are computed and stored in memory. For each subsequent decoding step, the model computes attention scores using the current query against the full cached K, and aggregates V values. The new token's K and V are then appended to the cache. Memory impact: for a 70B model with 80 layers and grouped-query attention using 8 KV heads of dimension 128, each token consumes 80 layers * (Key + Value) * 8 heads * 128 d * 2 bytes (FP16) = 327,680 bytes per token. A 128K context with batch size 4 requires about 160 GB of HBM just for the cache. Key trade-offs: sliding window attention restricts the cache to a fixed recent window, trading recall of early context for memory bounds; multi-query and grouped-query attention reduce the KV head count relative to query heads, cutting cache size by 4-8x in exchange for some quality loss; PagedAttention (vLLM) manages the cache in page blocks to avoid fragmentation and enable shared prefix caching; KV cache quantization (FP8, INT4) roughly halves memory at minor perplexity cost. Production serving systems must balance cache capacity against batch size and context length to maximize throughput without exceeding device memory.
explanation: The KV cache is not an optimization—it is a requirement for any viable transformer serving system. Without it, models with 128K context windows would be thousands of times slower and infeasible to deploy. Its importance grows superlinearly with context length because cache memory scales with (batch × layers × heads × dim), so doubling context doubles cache and also increases attention computation quadratically if not cached. Edge cases: position encoding schemes like RoPE require careful handling—if a shared KV cache prefix is reused across requests with different suffix lengths, the RoPE frequencies applied to the cached portion become stale, forcing recomputation. Speculative decoding introduces another complication: when a draft model's predictions are rejected, the KV cache must be rolled back to a previous state. Real-world usage: Llama 3 uses grouped-query attention (8 KV heads for 64 query heads) to shrink cache by 8x versus full multi-head attention; Mistral uses sliding window attention with a 4096-token window; DeepSeek employs multi-head latent attention to compress KV projections into a low-rank space. Serving frameworks like vLLM, TensorRT-LLM, and SGLang each implement custom cache managers that support prefix sharing, page-level allocation, and offloading to CPU memory for extremely long contexts where HBM is insufficient.
diagram:
flowchart TD
  A[LLM receives prompt] --> B[Prefill: compute K,V for all prompt tokens]
  B --> C[Store prompt K,V in KV cache]
  C --> D[Decode: generate first output token using cached K,V]
  D --> E[Compute new K,V for generated token]
  E --> F[Append new K,V to existing cache]
  F --> G{More tokens needed?}
  G -- Yes --> D
  G -- No --> H[Return complete sequence]

question: You are building a customer support chatbot for a SaaS company like Stripe. The knowledge base is updated weekly. How would you design a basic RAG pipeline to keep the chatbot grounded in the latest documentation, and what is the single most critical failure mode you would guard against?
answer: A production RAG pipeline for a SaaS customer support system has three critical phases: Ingestion, Retrieval, and Generation. During Ingestion, each documentation page is parsed using a document parser that identifies structure like headers and code blocks. The text is split into overlapping chunks of roughly 512 tokens with a 128-token overlap. Each chunk is converted into a dense vector embedding using a model like text-embedding-3-small and inserted into a vector database alongside metadata including source URL, version, and timestamp. When a user submits a query, the Retrieval phase embeds the query using the same model and performs an Approximate Nearest Neighbor search against the vector index. For SaaS products with specific API terminologies like idempotency keys, a pure vector search can fail. Therefore, we implement Hybrid Search, combining the vector cosine similarity score with a BM25 keyword score. The top 20 results from this hybrid search are passed to a Cross-Encoder Re-Ranker, which deeply scores each chunk against the query. We set a strict relevance threshold. If no chunk scores above the threshold, the pipeline bypasses the LLM entirely and returns a fallback response or escalates to a human agent. The single most critical failure mode is retrieval failure leading to hallucination. If the retriever returns irrelevant chunks, the LLM is forced to generate false information. The Re-Ranker with a strict cutoff directly solves this by preventing poor context from reaching the LLM. If chunks pass the threshold, the top 3-5 are injected into the LLM prompt with a strict grounding instruction: Answer using only the provided context. The LLM generates the final response. Monitoring requires tracking retrieval precision, re-ranker scores, and the frequency of fallback triggers to ensure the system stays reliable.
explanation: RAG fundamentally addresses the core limitation of purely generative LLMs: they have fixed, outdated parametric knowledge and no inherent access to private or real-time data. Without RAG, a chatbot might describe a deprecated API version or hallucinate a feature. The engineering discipline of RAG lies entirely in retrieval quality. Garbage in, garbage out applies perfectly. The re-rank stage is more important than the initial ANN search because cross-encoder re-rankers execute a deep semantic comparison between the query and each candidate chunk, scoring relevance on a scale that allows a hard cutoff. This prevents the LLM from seeing irrelevant context, which is a primary driver of hallucination in RAG systems. Edge cases are abundant: chunking too small loses context, too large dilutes relevance, and splitting a table destroys meaning. Metadata filtering on dates or product versions prevents retrieving deprecated content. Real-world usage demands a robust evaluation framework like RAGAS to track Faithfulness and Answer Relevancy. A pipeline that answers How do I cancel my account? might score high on generic evaluations but silently fail if the linked documentation is broken. The industry is moving towards Agentic RAG, where the LLM decides when to retrieve and which tools to use, demanding even more rigorous guardrails against cascading retrieval failures. The key insight is that RAG is an information retrieval problem first and an LLM problem second. A strong ranker and a robust fallback strategy are far more impactful than prompt engineering.
diagram:
flowchart TD
  A[User Query: How do I set up webhooks?] --> B[Query Embedding Model]
  B --> C[Hybrid Search: ANN + BM25]
  C --> D[Top 20 Candidate Chunks]
  D --> E[Cross-Encoder Re-Ranker]
  E --> F{Any Chunk Score > Threshold?}
  F -- Yes --> G[Top 5 Passed to LLM Context]
  F -- No --> H[Fallback: Route to Human Agent]
  G --> I[LLM Generates Answer from Context]
  I --> J[Final Grounded Response]
  H --> K[Create Escalation Ticket]

question: How would you design a retrieval-augmented generation system to handle queries that require synthesizing information from multiple documents with conflicting claims?
answer: To handle conflicting claims across multiple documents, the system must implement a multi-stage pipeline that explicitly detects and resolves contradictions. First, chunk documents with overlapping windows (e.g., 512 tokens with 128 overlap) to maximize context capture while maintaining chunk-level granularity for retrieval. Use a dense retriever like Contriever or ColBERT-v2 with a cross-encoder re-ranker (e.g., Cohere Rerank 3) to surface the top-20 most relevant passages. Next, employ a clustering step that groups retrieved chunks by aspect or stance using embedding similarity and k-means (k=number of distinct claims). For each cluster, prompt an LLM (e.g., GPT-4 or Claude) to generate a neutral summary, flagging any internal inconsistencies. Then, create a conflict graph where edges represent contradictory statements between clusters, weighted by source authority and recency metadata. The LLM is prompted with the graph, asked to assess evidence quality (source credibility, publication date, supporting studies) and to determine a synthesized position or explicitly note the controversy. If consensus exists, the final answer includes the agreed-upon statement with citations. If conflict remains, the answer presents both sides with confidence scores and source links, e.g., 'According to [2023 study], X holds; however, [2024 update] suggests Y.' This design prevents hallucinations by forcing the model to ground its output in retrieved evidence while transparently handling disagreements. Caching and pre-clustering frequent query patterns can reduce latency. For real-time systems, a streaming version processes clusters in parallel as chunks arrive.
explanation: This question tests understanding of RAG beyond naive retrieve-and-generate. Real production systems (e.g., at Uber for incident triage, at Stripe for policy compliance) must synthesize contradictory information from different versions of documentation, user reports, and external sources. A naive approach that concatenates retrieved chunks into one prompt often causes the LLM to hallucinate a false consensus or silently ignore evidence. The clustering step is critical because it groups evidence by stance, enabling the system to detect conflicts algorithmically rather than relying solely on LLM reasoning, which can be brittle. Edge cases include handling stale data (e.g., an old document contradicts a newer one, where recency should be preferred) and multiple valid perspectives (e.g., regulatory interpretations from different jurisdictions). The conflict graph approach mirrors how humans compare evidence—by weighing authority, recency, and corroboration—and provides a structured fallback when the LLM cannot resolve disagreements. It also enables auditability: each answer cites the specific sources and explains why one view was prioritized. Companies like Airbnb use similar architectures for listing summarization, where reviews and host descriptions may contradict. A key trade-off is latency: clustering and graph construction add 200-500ms per query, which can be mitigated by pre-computing clusters for static corpora or limiting re-clustering to a sliding window of the most recent documents. Without such design, RAG systems risk producing confidently wrong answers.
diagram:
flowchart TD
  A[Query Input] --> B[Retrieve Top-20 Chunks]
  B --> C[Cluster Chunks by Aspect/Stance]
  C --> D[Build Conflict Graph with Weights]
  D --> E{Consensus?}
  E -->|Yes| F[Generate Answer with Citations]
  E -->|No| G[Present Both Sides with Confidence Scores]

question: You are building a text classification system for a product with high accuracy requirements and limited labeled data. Compare the approaches of fine-tuning a BERT-sized model vs. prompting GPT-4 with few-shot examples. What factors would influence your decision?
answer: This question tests understanding of two core paradigms: fine-tuning (transfer learning) and in-context learning (prompting). Fine-tuning a BERT-sized encoder model takes the pre-trained weights and adapts them to the target task using labeled examples. The model updates its parameters through supervised learning, typically requiring 1k-10k labeled examples for good performance. Inference is fast, cheap, and can run on a single GPU or CPU, making it viable for high-throughput production. Accuracy is generally higher for narrow, well-defined tasks because the model learns a dedicated decision boundary. However, it requires maintaining separate model versions, deploying new artifacts for each task, and cannot easily incorporate new information at inference time. In-context learning with GPT-4 requires no parameter updates—you provide task instructions and a few examples in the prompt. This is ideal when labeled data is extremely scarce, the task is dynamic, or you need to quickly iterate on task definitions. GPT-4's massive pre-training enables strong generalization from very few examples (5-50). The downsides include higher per-query cost (especially at scale), higher latency (hundreds of milliseconds to seconds), context window constraints limiting the number of examples, and weaker performance on niche or highly specific domain tasks where the model lacks pre-training exposure. Inference also requires network connectivity to an API, introducing dependency and privacy considerations. The decision hinges on data availability: with hundreds of labeled examples, fine-tuning often wins on accuracy and cost. With a handful of examples or a rapidly changing task, few-shot prompting is more practical. Latency requirements: if sub-10ms inference is needed, fine-tuning is mandatory. For batch jobs or moderate latency tolerance, prompting works. Cost structure: at low volume, prompting is cheaper (no MLOps); at high volume, fine-tuning becomes dramatically cheaper per prediction. Privacy and compliance: fine-tuning on-premises keeps data in-house; sending sensitive data to an API may be prohibited. Finally, consider maintenance: fine-tuned models drift as the data distribution shifts and require retraining; prompting can be adapted by editing the prompt, offering faster iteration cycles.
explanation: This question reveals whether a candidate understands the fundamental trade-off between learned parameters and emergent in-context abilities—a decision every applied ML team faces. The key insight is that fine-tuning compresses task knowledge into model weights, enabling efficient inference but creating a static artifact that must be rebuilt when the task changes. In-context learning keeps the model frozen and shifts knowledge into the prompt, making it flexible but computationally expensive per token. Edge cases emerge when the classification task involves nuanced domain expertise—e.g., medical code assignment or legal contract clauses. Fine-tuned BERT-sized models often struggle with these unless trained on large in-domain datasets, whereas GPT-4 may have seen similar examples during pre-training and perform well with minimal examples. Another edge case is multi-label classification with hundreds of possible labels: fine-tuning can handle this with a properly designed output head, while using few-shot prompting may exceed context windows or dilute label representation. Real-world usage at companies like Stripe and Uber demonstrates this: fraud detection often uses fine-tuned transformer models for latency-sensitive scoring, while support ticket routing might use LLM prompting to handle rapidly changing categories. At Airbnb, listing classification for policy compliance uses fine-tuning for high-volume checks, but edge cases and appeals route to a prompted LLM for nuanced reasoning. The best systems often combine both—a fine-tuned model as a primary classifier and a prompted LLM as an arbiter for low-confidence predictions.
diagram:
flowchart TD
  A[Start: Classification Task] --> B{Labeled Data Available?}
  B -->|Hundreds+| C[Fine-tune BERT-sized Model]
  B -->|Handful| D[Use GPT-4 Few-Shot Prompting]
  C --> E{Latency < 10ms?}
  E -->|Yes| F[Deploy Fine-tuned Model for Inference]
  E -->|No| G[Consider Prompting if Cost Allows]
  D --> H{Data Sensitive?}
  H -->|Yes| I[Fine-tune Open Model On-Premises]
  H -->|No| J[Use API with Prompt Engineering]
  F --> K[High-Volume Production Ready]
  G --> K
  I --> L[Privacy-Compliant Custom Model]
  J --> M[Flexible, Quick to Iterate]
  K --> N[Monitor for Drift & Retrain]
  M --> O[Adapt Prompt as Task Evolves]

question: Design a multi-agent orchestration system where a router agent delegates subtasks to specialized agents, each of which can call external tools. How do you handle agent failures, ambiguous delegation, and tool call errors while maintaining coherent state across the conversation?
answer: The system uses a supervisor pattern with a router agent that maintains a shared state object tracking conversation context, subtask status, and tool results. Each specialized agent receives a self-contained prompt with the relevant context slice and a strict tool-use schema. For failure handling: (1) Tool call errors trigger an automatic retry with exponential backoff up to 3 attempts. (2) If the specialized agent produces invalid output or fails after retries, the router logs the failure, marks the subtask as degraded, and either falls back to a simpler approach or prompts the user for clarification. (3) Ambiguous delegation is handled via a confidence threshold — if the router's classification logits fall below 0.7, it explicitly asks the user rather than guessing. State coherence is maintained via a JSON state machine that tracks all conversation turns, pending tool calls, resolved outputs, and dependency graph of subtasks. Each agent's completion includes a "stateDelta" field that the router applies to the global state, keeping the state authoritative. The router also runs a consistency check after each transition: verifying no required subtask remains pending, no circular dependencies exist, and the accumulated token count stays under context limits. For long-running workflows, the state is checkpointed to a durable store (Redis with TTL) so failures can recover from the last consistent snapshot rather than restarting. Error propagation is contained: a failure in one specialized agent does not cascade — the router marks only the dependent path as degraded while other branches continue. This mirrors patterns used in production systems like LangGraph's StateGraph with checkpointing and Amazon's Step Functions for agentic workflows.
explanation: This question tests understanding of distributed systems principles applied to LLM orchestration — a critical skill as agent deployments move from demos to production. The core challenge is that LLMs are inherently non-deterministic: the same prompt can produce different tool calls, different reasoning paths, or hallucinations. A well-designed orchestration layer must treat each LLM call as an unreliable actor, similar to how distributed systems handle flaky microservices. The state machine approach with checkpointing mirrors real production patterns at scale. For example, Google's Agent-to-Agent protocol uses structured delegation with shared schemas, and Meta's agents use a manager pattern with explicit error contracts. The ambiguity handling (confidence thresholds, user-in-the-loop) is directly inspired by Anthropic's research on tool-use reliability showing that forcing a decision on low-confidence classifications leads to 40% more recovery actions. Real-world edge cases include: an agent calling a tool that returns a schema mismatch, an agent going silent mid-execution (timeout), or two specialized agents producing contradictory outputs that need reconciliation. Companies like Amazon handle this with a centralized orchestrator (AWS Step Functions + Bedrock Agents) that enforces timeouts, retries, and compensation actions. The diagram below captures the control flow with the router, specialized agents, external tools, error handling branches, and the shared state persistence layer.
diagram:
flowchart TD
  A[User Input] --> B[Router Agent]
  B --> C{Confidence > 0.7?}
  C -->|Yes| D[Decompose to Subtasks]
  C -->|No| E[Ask User for Clarification]
  E --> A
  D --> F[Assign to Specialized Agent 1]
  D --> G[Assign to Specialized Agent 2]
  F --> H[Call External Tool]
  G --> I[Call External Tool]
  H --> J{Tool Success?}
  I --> K{Tool Success?}
  J -->|Yes| L[Apply State Delta]
  J -->|No| M[Retry x3 with Backoff]
  M --> H
  K -->|Yes| N[Apply State Delta]
  K -->|No| O[Retry x3 with Backoff]
  O --> I
  L --> P[Consistency Check]
  N --> P
  P --> Q{All Checks Pass?}
  Q -->|Yes| R[Merge to Global State]
  Q -->|No| S[Mark Degraded Path / Recover]
  S --> R
  R --> T[Checkpoint to Redis]
  T --> U[Respond to User]

question: You are fine-tuning a 7B parameter model to replace a 70B parameter teacher model for a high-volume, latency-sensitive intent classification task at Uber Eats. The output must be a deeply nested JSON structure representing the user's issue. Walk through your exact pipeline for generating training data from the teacher, the specific fine-tuning method you would use and why, and how you would rigorously validate that the student model has genuinely learned the underlying logic rather than simply memorizing the training examples?
answer: My pipeline starts with high-quality, diverse data. I do not just use raw teacher outputs. First, I stratify historical support tickets by intent category to ensure balanced representation. For each ticket, I query the teacher model multiple times (e.g., 5) with the same strict JSON schema prompt. I apply a self-consistency filter, keeping only examples where the majority of outputs agree. This filters out ambiguous cases that would add noise. Next, I synthesize adversarial examples: slight paraphrases that should map to the same intent, and confuser examples (similar slot values but different intents). This teaches the decision boundary, not just a lookup table. For fine-tuning, I use LoRA applied to all attention projection layers (q_proj, v_proj, k_proj, o_proj). LoRA is parameter-efficient and preserves the base model's broad language understanding (slang, typos, multilingual queries common at Uber Eats). I mix in 5-10% general instruction-following data to prevent catastrophic forgetting of the base model's capabilities. To validate genuine learning, I use three strategies. First, a held-out set of intents completely unseen during training. The model must output a high-confidence unknown rather than hallucinating. Second, counterfactual evaluation: I systematically perturb named entities and verb tense in the inputs and verify the output JSON remains stable. Third, I compute representational similarity between the student's internal embeddings and the teacher's for the held-out set. High similarity indicates the student has learned the teacher's reasoning pathway, not just the final token distributions. Finally, the model deploys in shadow mode, with human agents reviewing 1% of traffic online, tracking precision and recall per intent with a statistical significance gate against the teacher's performance.
explanation: This question tests the reconciliation of theoretical ML knowledge with hard production constraints like latency, cost, and robustness. The core tension between distillation and generalization is a common challenge at companies like Stripe, Uber, and Airbnb. The self-consistency data filter is critical because raw teacher logprobs do not fully capture output uncertainty for generative models. Naively using all teacher outputs introduces noise that the student will overfit on. The adversarial and confuser data explicitly teaches the model the decision boundary, preventing it from relying on spurious correlations. The choice of LoRA over full fine-tuning is deliberate. Full fine-tuning of a 7B model risks catastrophic forgetting of foundational language capabilities (e.g., understanding the highly varied user input common in customer support). LoRA constrains the weight update to a low-rank space, preserving general knowledge. The evaluation strategy demonstrates genuine depth beyond simple hold-out accuracy. The held-out intent test directly probes whether the model has learned the schema layer, not just memorized label names. The hidden layer similarity metric (Representational Similarity Analysis) is a sophisticated technique for verifying the student mimics the teacher's reasoning pathway, not just its token distribution. In production at these companies, this multi-faceted evaluation is essential to prevent subtle regressions that would erode user trust.
diagram:
flowchart TD
  A[Historical Tickets] --> B[Stratify by Intent]
  B --> C[Teacher Distillation 5x per sample]
  C --> D[Self-Consistency Filter]
  D --> E[Adversarial and Confuser Synthesis]
  E --> F[LoRA Fine-Tuning 7B Model]
  F --> G[Held-Out Intent Eval]
  F --> H[Counterfactual Robustness Test]
  F --> I[Representational Similarity vs Teacher]
  G --> J[Shadow Deploy 1% Traffic]
  H --> J
  I --> J

question: In transformer-based LLMs, why does the softmax attention mechanism require causal masking during autoregressive generation, and how would the output distribution change if the mask were incorrectly set to allow future token attention?
answer: During autoregressive generation, a transformer LLM produces one token at a time, where each new token depends only on previously generated tokens. The causal mask (an upper-triangular matrix filled with -infinity above the diagonal) ensures that when computing attention scores via softmax, each token position can only attend to itself and preceding positions. This preserves the autoregressive property that defines next-token prediction. If the mask were removed—allowing bidirectional attention—every token position would attend to all other positions including future tokens that haven't been generated yet. During training, this is acceptable (masked language models like BERT use bidirectional attention), but during autoregressive generation, it would leak information from future positions. For a concrete example: when generating the third token, the model would see the yet-unwritten fourth token's embedding (typically a padding or placeholder token). The softmax over all positions would assign some probability mass to this invalid future position, skewing the distribution. The generated output would no longer be a valid conditional probability P(token_n | tokens_1..n-1) but rather an invalid conditional that depends on information that doesn't exist at decode time. Empirically, this causes the model to produce incoherent output because the attention distribution is diluted across positions it shouldn't know about. In grouped-query attention (GQA) and multi-query attention variants used in models like Llama 2/3 and Mistral, the same causal masking logic applies identically—the mask operates on the query-key dot products before softmax, regardless of how many key-value heads exist. The key insight is that causal masking isn't an optimization; it's a correctness requirement for autoregressive decoding.
explanation: This question tests understanding of the fundamental architectural constraint that makes autoregressive generation work. The causal mask is often glossed over in high-level explanations of transformers, but its implementation details matter in production. Edge cases include: (1) Speculative decoding—where draft models must use causal masks correctly while the target model verifies multiple tokens; an incorrect mask here breaks the rejection sampling guarantees. (2) Prefix caching—where a precomputed KV cache from a shared prompt must respect the same causal ordering; reusing KV caches with different masks leads to silent correctness bugs. (3) Fill-in-the-middle (FIM) training used by Code LLMs like Codex and StarCoder—where causal masking is modified to allow bidirectional context in certain regions, requiring careful mask engineering. In production systems at Microsoft and Netflix, causal mask bugs have caused model-serving incidents where inference servers produced garbage output after cache updates or batch recomputation. Real-world implementations in vLLM, TensorRT-LLM, and Hugging Face Transformers all handle masking differently: Flash Attention fuses the mask into the attention kernel, PagedAttention stores mask metadata per block, and continuous batching requires dynamic mask recomputation as sequences complete at different times. Understanding this mechanism is essential for debugging generation quality issues, optimizing inference throughput via KV cache management, and correctly implementing techniques like parallel decoding or Medusa-style multi-token prediction.
diagram:
flowchart TD
  A[Autoregressive Decoding Starts] --> B[Input: tokens_1..n-1]
  B --> C[Compute Q, K, V projections]
  C --> D[Compute attentions Q*K^T]
  D --> E[Apply causal mask: upper-triangle -inf]
  E --> F[Softmax: row-wise normalization]
  F --> G[Weighted sum with V]
  G --> H[Generate next token logits]
  H --> I{Sequence complete?}
  I -->|No| J[Append new token to input]
  J --> B
  I -->|Yes| K[Return full sequence]
  C --> L[KV Cache: store K,V tensors]
  L --> M[Next step uses cached K,V + new Q]
  M --> D
